content.text: Text-to-speech has gone through three distinct generations in the deep learning era. Autoregressive encoder-decoder models established that neural networks could match human voice quality. Non-autoregressive models traded naturalness for speed and explicit prosody control. End-to-end and codec-language-model approaches removed the mel-spectrogram bottleneck entirely, producing more expressive and speaker-flexible synthesis. Understanding the trade-offs of each architecture helps you choose the right model for a given deployment context.

attrs.alt: TTS model landscape timeline: Tacotron 2 (2018, autoregressive seq2seq), Glow-TTS (2020, normalising flow), FastSpeech 2 and VITS and Grad-TTS (2021), YourTTS (2022), NaturalSpeech 2 and Bark (2023), Parler-TTS and CosyVoice (2024)

content.text: Google's Tacotron 2 (2018) set the quality standard that later models competed against. Its architecture is a sequence-to-sequence model with three components:
A stack of convolutional layers followed by a bidirectional LSTM converts the input character/phoneme sequence into a context-rich hidden sequence.

Location-sensitive attention aligns the decoder's current position to encoder tokens. The attention mechanism is the critical component: it must learn a monotone left-to-right alignment over characters without explicit supervision. When attention fails (word repetition, skipping), the output becomes garbled.

A 2-layer LSTM generates mel-spectrogram frames one at a time, each conditioned on the previous frame. A PostNet (5-layer 1D conv) refines the full mel sequence after decoding. A separate stop-token classifier predicts when to halt.
The WaveNet vocoder was originally paired with Tacotron 2 but has been replaced by HiFi-GAN in most modern deployments, reducing inference time from minutes to milliseconds per utterance.

Autoregressive decoding means errors compound: the model sees its own imperfect mel output as the input for the next frame. Long or unusual sequences (rare names, numbers) cause attention drift. This is the primary motivation for non-autoregressive approaches.

FastSpeech 2 (Microsoft, 2021) resolved Tacotron 2's robustness and speed problems by removing the autoregressive loop entirely. A Feed-Forward Transformer (FFT) processes all input tokens in parallel. The key innovation is the Variance Adaptor between the phoneme encoder and mel decoder, which explicitly models the three prosodic dimensions — duration, pitch (F0), and energy — using lightweight convolutional predictors.

Because the Length Regulator expands phoneme representations to frame resolution non-autoregressively, the model produces the entire mel-spectrogram in a single decoder forward pass. This achieves roughly 200× real-time on a GPU — synthesis of a 10-second sentence completes in 50 ms.

# FastSpeech 2 inference via Coqui TTS
from TTS.api import TTS

tts = TTS(model_name="tts_models/en/ljspeech/fast_pitch")

# Basic synthesis
tts.tts_to_file(
    text="Synthesis completes in a single decoder pass.",
    file_path="output_fs2.wav"
)

# With prosody control
tts.tts_to_file(
    text="Speed and pitch are independently controllable.",
    file_path="output_fs2_mod.wav",
    speed=0.85,                              # 15% slower
    pitch_transform=lambda f0: f0 * 2**(3/12)  # +3 semitones
)

FastSpeech 2 requires forced alignments from a Montreal Forced Aligner or a pre-trained autoregressive model to supervise its duration predictor. Getting clean alignments for rare words or accented speech is non-trivial. FastPitch (NVIDIA) extends it with per-phoneme F0 prediction and has become the dominant production variant.
VITS: End-to-End Without a Mel Bottleneck

VITS (Variational Inference with adversarial learning for end-to-end Text-to-Speech, Kakao Brain, 2021) demonstrated that synthesising directly from text to waveform without ever constructing an intermediate mel-spectrogram produces measurably more natural output. The architecture fuses three components:
A posterior encoder (run only during training) encodes the ground-truth waveform into a latent
. A prior encoder predicts the same latent from text only. The KL divergence between prior and posterior drives the text encoder to model the full audio distribution.

A series of invertible coupling layers transforms the latent into a waveform space. This makes the latent-to-audio mapping deterministic and invertible, which is necessary for using maximum-likelihood training jointly with the VAE.
The waveform is refined by a HiFi-GAN-style generator, trained with MPD and MSD discriminators to ensure perceptual realism at the fine-grained waveform level.

Duration is handled by Monotonic Alignment Search (MAS): a differentiable dynamic programming algorithm that finds the most likely phoneme-to-latent alignment jointly during training, requiring no pre-computed forced alignments.

# VITS inference via Coqui TTS
from TTS.api import TTS

tts = TTS(model_name="tts_models/en/ljspeech/vits")
tts.tts_to_file(
    text="End-to-end synthesis without a mel bottleneck.",
    file_path="vits_output.wav"
)

# VITS--multi-speaker variant (speaker embedding)
tts_ms = TTS(model_name="tts_models/en/vctk/vits")
tts_ms.tts_to_file(
    text="Each speaker has a unique voice.",
    speaker="p225",           # VCTK speaker ID
    file_path="vits_speaker_p225.wav"
)

Grad-TTS (Kakao, 2021) and DiffTTS applied score-based diffusion models to the mel-spectrogram. The model learns to reverse a Gaussian diffusion process conditioned on the text encoding. Given enough denoising steps, diffusion TTS produces extremely natural output — often rated higher than autoregressive models by human evaluators — but at 50–1000 denoising steps it was initially too slow for real-time deployment.

DiffSpeech and FastDiff addressed the speed problem using deterministic ODE solvers (DDIM) that reduce steps to 4–10 with negligible quality loss. At 4 steps, diffusion TTS reaches near-real-time performance on a GPU while preserving quality advantages.

NaturalSpeech 2 (Microsoft, 2023) scaled diffusion to the codec domain: instead of diffusing over mel, it diffuses over the continuous latent codes of a neural audio codec (EnCodec). This decouples the vocoder entirely and makes the diffusion target smoother and more compressible, improving both quality and convergence speed.
Codec Language Models: Bark and Parler-TTS

The third generation of TTS drops the structured acoustic pipeline altogether and treats speech synthesis as language modelling over discrete codec tokens. This mirrors the approach of large language models but operates on audio codec codebooks rather than text vocabulary.
Bark is a transformer-based model that autoregressively generates EnCodec codec tokens in three stages:

A GPT-style model converts text (plus an optional speaker history prompt) into semantic tokens derived from a self-supervised audio model (HuBERT/w2v-BERT). These capture phonetic content and prosodic phrasing at an abstract level.
A second transformer predicts the first two residual quantiser (RQ) codebooks of EnCodec from the semantic tokens. These determine coarse spectral structure and speaker identity.
A third (smaller) transformer predicts the remaining RQ codebooks, which add fine-grained waveform detail. The full codec token sequence is decoded by EnCodec to produce the final waveform.

Because Bark is a language model over audio tokens, it inherits LM capabilities: it can synthesise laughter, sighs, pauses, background noise, and music by treating them as part of the token vocabulary. It has no explicit prosody API — prosody emerges from the language model's predictions.

from bark import SAMPLE_RATE, generate_audio, preload_models
from scipy.io.wavfile import write as write_wav
import numpy as np

preload_models()  # downloads ~5 GB of model weights on first run

# Speaker prompt: 'v2/en_speaker_6' selects a specific voice profile
prompt = """[clears throat] Hello, and welcome to the tutorial.
[laughs] I think you'll find this quite interesting."""

audio_array = generate_audio(prompt, history_prompt="v2/en_speaker_6")
write_wav("bark_output.wav", SAMPLE_RATE, audio_array.astype(np.float32))

Parler-TTS makes style conditioning explicit with a text description that specifies the desired voice characteristics. You describe the voice in natural language and the model synthesises accordingly:

from parler_tts import ParlerTTSForConditionalGeneration
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
import soundfile as sf
import torch

device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
model = ParlerTTSForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained("parler-tts/parler-tts-mini-v1").to(device)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("parler-tts/parler-tts-mini-v1")

# The description controls voice style -- no speaker audio clip needed
description = "A calm female speaker with a slightly low pitch, slow pace, and warm tone."
text = "Generative AI is reshaping how we interact with machines."

desc_ids = tokenizer(description, return_tensors="pt").input_ids.to(device)
prompt_ids = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt").input_ids.to(device)

generation = model.generate(input_ids=desc_ids, prompt_input_ids=prompt_ids)
audio = generation.cpu().numpy().squeeze()
sf.write("parler_output.wav", audio, model.config.sampling_rate)

YourTTS and XTTS v2 (Coqui) extend VITS for zero-shot speaker cloning: the model encodes a short reference clip (3–10 seconds) into a speaker embedding that conditions synthesis. The target voice need not have appeared in training data.

from TTS.api import TTS

# XTTS v2: zero-shot cloning from a reference clip
tts = TTS(model_name="tts_models/multilingual/multi-dataset/xtts_v2")

tts.tts_to_file(
    text="This is synthesised in your voice.",
    speaker_wav="reference_clip.wav",   # 3-10 seconds of target speaker
    language="en",
    file_path="cloned_output.wav"
)

XTTS v2 supports streaming synthesis — generating audio in 100 ms chunks as text is produced by an LLM — making it suitable for low-latency conversational agents. CosyVoice (Alibaba, 2024) uses a flow-matching latent model with the same zero-shot capability and extends to cross-lingual voice cloning, where the target speaker voice is applied to a different language than the reference clip.
Glow-TTS: Normalising Flows for Parallel Synthesis

Glow-TTS (Kakao Brain, 2020) was the first non-autoregressive model to achieve Tacotron 2 quality without pre-computed alignments. It uses Monotonic Alignment Search (MAS) -- the same differentiable dynamic programming algorithm later adopted by VITS -- to find the most probable monotone alignment between text and mel frames during training. The acoustic model is a normalising flow: a sequence of invertible affine coupling layers that maps a simple Gaussian prior conditioned on text to a mel-spectrogram.

At inference, a sample from the Gaussian prior is passed through the inverse flow to produce the mel-spectrogram. A temperature hyperparameter on the prior variance controls stochasticity: lower temperature produces more stable, conservative prosody; higher temperature produces more expressive but less consistent output. Because the model is fully parallel (no autoregressive step), inference is approximately 10x faster than Tacotron 2 on equivalent hardware.

Glow-TTS was superseded by VITS shortly after publication -- VITS combines the MAS alignment idea with a VAE and adversarial decoder that produces waveforms directly, making the separate vocoder step unnecessary. However, Glow-TTS remains instructive as a clean example of applying normalising flows to a structured prediction problem.

Training a TTS model from scratch on a new voice requires hours of clean recorded speech (typically 10-50 hours for a high-quality single-speaker model). Fine-tuning a pre-trained model dramatically reduces this requirement to 5-30 minutes of new voice data.
For FastSpeech 2, the typical fine-tuning workflow is:

Record 30-minute clips of the target speaker reading phonetically diverse sentences. Normalise audio to -23 LUFS, trim silence, and split into 2-10 second segments. Run MFA forced alignment to generate TextGrid files.
Extract F0 using harvest/pyin and compute per-speaker mean and standard deviation in log space. These normalisation constants are stored in the dataset configuration.

Load a pre-trained checkpoint and fine-tune all weights (or only the decoder + speaker embedding if data is scarce). 1,000-5,000 steps at 1/10 the original learning rate typically suffices for a new voice.

XTTS v2 exposes a fine-tuning pipeline that only updates the speaker conditioning layers, keeping the language model weights frozen. This is important because the language model backbone is responsible for language coverage and pronunciation rules -- corrupting it with limited-data fine-tuning would harm multilingual quality.

# XTTS v2 fine-tuning via Coqui TTS CLI
# Prepare metadata.csv: each line is filename|text|speaker

# Step 1: preprocess (extract mel features, speaker embed)
tts train --config_path /path/to/xtts_config.json \
          --restore_path xtts_v2.pth \
          --coqpit-overrides max_audio_len=255995 \
          output_path=./fine_tuned/

# Step 2: synthesise with the fine-tuned model
tts synthesize \
  --model_path ./fine_tuned/best_model.pth \
  --config_path ./fine_tuned/config.json \
  --speaker_wav reference.wav \
  --language en \
  --text "Fine-tuning adapts the model to your voice."
After fine-tuning, evaluate on a held-out set of sentences not seen during training. Key checks:
Human raters compare the synthesised voice to the reference speaker. Target >= 3.5/5.
Run ASR on the output and compare against the input text. Over-fitting to a small dataset can cause the model to drop or repeat words on out-of-domain inputs.

Compare speaker embeddings of synthesised and reference audio using a pre-trained speaker verification model (e.g. WeSpeaker). Target cosine similarity >= 0.85 indicates successful voice identity transfer.
The right model depends on deployment constraints and product requirements:
NaturalSpeech 2 or XTTS v2 for English. Both produce human-comparable quality on standard benchmarks.
FastPitch (FastSpeech 2 variant) with HiFi-GAN. Explicit prosody controls, 200x real-time, stable under unusual inputs.
XTTS v2 (open-source, multilingual) or CosyVoice (cross-lingual). Both work from 3-second reference clips.
Bark. The only open-source model that synthesises laughter, music, and sound effects alongside speech within the same generation pass.
Azure Neural TTS, Google Cloud TTS (WaveNet/Neural2), or Amazon Polly. All expose SSML for prosody control and support dozens of neural voices.

Every TTS pipeline begins with a text front-end that converts raw input text into a phoneme sequence. This step is more critical than it might appear: the acoustic model has no linguistic knowledge of how to pronounce text it has not seen in this exact form. Text normalisation and phonemisation ensure that the model receives a consistent, unambiguous input.
Text normalisation (TN) expands non-standard words (NSW) into their spoken form before phonemisation. Examples of NSW categories:
https colon slash slash example dot com

After normalisation, grapheme-to-phoneme (G2P) conversion maps text characters to phoneme symbols from a standard alphabet (ARPAbet for American English, IPA for language-independent systems). Most production TTS systems use a lexicon-lookup approach for dictionary words combined with a G2P neural model for out-of-vocabulary (OOV) terms:

from phonemizer import phonemize
from phonemizer.backend import EspeakBackend

# G2P using espeak-ng backend (open source, 100+ languages)
backend = EspeakBackend(language='en-us', preserve_punctuation=True, with_stress=True)

text = "The researcher presented her findings at MIT."
phonemes = backend.phonemize([text], separator=None)
# Returns: [['DH', 'AH0', ' ', 'R', 'IY0', 'S', ...]]
print(phonemes[0][:10])  # ['DH', 'AH0', ' ', 'R', 'IY0', 'S', 'ER1', 'CH', 'ER0', ' ']

# Note: 'MIT' is correctly expanded to 'EH2 M AY1 T IY1' (letter-by-letter)
# The text normaliser must have already expanded it before G2P

In modern end-to-end TTS systems like VITS or Bark, phonemisation can be applied at the input boundary, or the model can be trained end-to-end on character/byte inputs with sufficient data. Character models handle OOV words more gracefully but require larger models and more training data to generalise pronunciation rules.
Tacotron 2 established the quality baseline but suffers from attention drift and slow autoregressive decoding.
FastSpeech 2 achieves ~200x real-time with explicit pitch, duration, and energy control, at the cost of requiring forced alignments during training.
VITS removes the mel-spectrogram bottleneck with VAE + flow + GAN, using MAS to jointly learn text-audio alignment.
Diffusion TTS offers the highest quality ceiling; DDIM fast-sampling brings it to near-real-time at 4 denoising steps.
Bark and Parler-TTS represent the codec-LM paradigm: language models over discrete audio tokens produce expressive, unconstrained synthesis without an explicit acoustic pipeline.
XTTS v2 and CosyVoice deliver zero-shot speaker cloning from 3-second reference clips with multilingual support and streaming output.
